rescue_reasoning: Keep because climate relevance is explicit at the parent level: parent states 'W7 reduces fugitive methane and co-pollutants...' and includes 'sewer transition assistance'. The sub-statement ('Provide financial assistance for sewer hookups...') is one of those measures. However, the dominant climate mechanism described is methane reduction (GHG mitigation), not resource efficiency; thus the primary category should be Mitigation.
policy_statement: Provide financial assistance for sewer hookups to defray high upfront sewering costs.
mechanism_description: The state provides financial assistance for sewer hookups to lower upfront costs and encourage more properties to connect to sewers, improving wastewater management and sanitation.
primary_causal_pathway: Waste stream management improvement → ↓ uncontrolled releases (incl. methane, nutrient pollution) + more efficient service provision
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changed activity/system: Property owners are incentivized (via subsidies/grants/low-cost financing) to connect buildings to centralized sewer networks instead of relying on septic systems, cesspools, or unmanaged discharge.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Wastewater is collected and treated under controlled conditions (primary/secondary/advanced treatment), reducing anaerobic decomposition in unmanaged settings and improving containment/processing of organics and nutrients.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Potential reduction in fugitive methane from unmanaged wastewater and improved efficiency/oversight of the wastewater stream; in some cases, treatment energy use may increase, but the mechanism’s intent is improved wastewater management performance.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is shifting wastewater from unmanaged/dispersed disposal to managed collection and treatment, which can reduce uncontrolled methane emissions from untreated wastewater (though the magnitude depends on baseline conditions and treatment type).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Financial support increases sewer connections → more wastewater handled in a managed system → reduced uncontrolled releases from unmanaged disposal (and generally improved efficiency/quality of wastewater management).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: It is not primarily an adaptation measure (not framed as hazard exposure reduction) and not inherently a nature-based solution. While it can yield mitigation co-benefits (methane reductions), those are not consistently the main or guaranteed outcome across all implementations; the consistent outcome is improved wastewater/waste-stream management.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the marginal connection causes large increases in electricity use at energy-intensive treatment without methane capture, net GHG could increase; conversely, if it replaces high-methane unmanaged lagoons or enables biogas capture, mitigation could be strong. These variations do not change the common mechanism of improved wastewater management.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances: Any policy that provides financial assistance for sewer hookups is fundamentally about increasing connection rates to improve wastewater collection/treatment management; that shared mechanism aligns best with Resource Efficiency as the cross-cutting primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a specific policy instance is net Mitigation-positive (or has adaptation/public-health emphasis) depends on baseline disposal practices, treatment technology, energy mix, and methane capture—requiring policy- and context-specific evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the policy is explicitly justified/implemented to cut methane from unmanaged wastewater (e.g., replacing open lagoons) it could reasonably be classified as Mitigation for that specific policy instance; if explicitly aimed at flood/resilience or overflow risk reduction it could lean Adaptation. For the canonical mechanism as written (financial assistance → improved wastewater management), Resource Efficiency is the most consistently applicable primary category.
key_indicators: “provide financial assistance”; “sewer hookups”; “defray high upfront costs”; “Encouraging transition to sewer”
co_benefits: Improved public health and sanitation; reduced local water contamination (nutrients/pathogens); potential property value/quality-of-life improvements.
instance_edge_case_notes: Climate impact depends on baseline (septic/cesspool/unmanaged discharge) and treatment plant energy/CH4 controls; text frames the instrument as encouragement (not a mandate), so classification is an incentive rather than regulatory.
